answer:
We can test the argument by trying to make the premises true and the conclusion false.  
If the conclusion \( L \supset (R \supset P) \) is false, then \( L \) is true, \( R \) is true, and \( P \) is false.  
With \( L \) true, \( R \) true, and \( P \) false, the premises force contradictions unless we assign truth values that satisfy them; the option with \( L, N, R \) true and \( M, O, P, Q \) false makes the premises true and conclusion false, showing invalidity.  

\boxed{D}
